Musical artist

**George Lawrence Thorogood** (born February 24, 1950) is an American musician, singer and songwriter.[[1]](#cite_note-Music-1) His "high-energy boogie-blues" sound became a staple of 1980s US rock radio, with hits like his original songs "[Bad to the Bone](/wiki/Bad_to_the_Bone "Bad to the Bone")" and "[I Drink Alone](/wiki/I_Drink_Alone "I Drink Alone")".[[2]](#cite_note-web_bio-2) He has also helped to popularize older songs by American icons, such as "[Move It on Over](/wiki/Move_It_On_Over_(song) "Move It On Over (song)")", "[Who Do You Love?](/wiki/Who_Do_You_Love%3F_(Bo_Diddley_song) "Who Do You Love? (Bo Diddley song)")", and "House Rent Blues/[One Bourbon, One Scotch, One Beer](/wiki/One_Bourbon,_One_Scotch,_One_Beer "One Bourbon, One Scotch, One Beer")".[[3]](#cite_note-3)

With his band, "The Delaware Destroyers" (often known simply as the "Destroyers"), Thorogood has released over 20 albums, two of which have been [certified Platinum](/wiki/RIAA_certification "RIAA certification") and six have been [certified Gold](/wiki/RIAA_certification "RIAA certification"). He has sold 15 million records worldwide. Thorogood and his band continue to tour extensively, and in 2024, the band celebrated their 50th anniversary of performing.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=George_Thorogood&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

George Lawrence Thorogood was born on February 24, 1950 in [Wilmington, Delaware](/wiki/Wilmington,_Delaware "Wilmington, Delaware").[[1]](#cite_note-Music-1) He attended [Brandywine High School](/wiki/Brandywine_High_School "Brandywine High School") where he met and became friends with future drummer and bandmate Jeff Simon.[[4]](#cite_note-:10-4) Thorogood showed interest in baseball at a young age, playing semi-professional baseball as a [second](/wiki/Second_baseman "Second baseman") and [first baseman](/wiki/First_baseman "First baseman").[[5]](#cite_note-gw-5) Thorogood first discovered slide guitar when a friend gifted him a slide for his birthday.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Music career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=George_Thorogood&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Music career")]

Thorogood began his career as a solo acoustic performer in the style of [Robert Johnson](/wiki/Robert_Johnson "Robert Johnson") and [Elmore James](/wiki/Elmore_James "Elmore James")[[2]](#cite_note-web_bio-2) in 1970, after being inspired by a [John P. Hammond](/wiki/John_P._Hammond "John P. Hammond") concert.[[7]](#cite_note-Erlewine-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:4-8) He traveled to [San Francisco, California](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco") and performed on the streets before a tape of one of his sets got into the hands of [Bonnie Raitt](/wiki/Bonnie_Raitt "Bonnie Raitt")'s manager. He convinced Thorogood to come back to the [East Coast](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States"). There he started playing with blues artists [Sonny Terry](/wiki/Sonny_Terry "Sonny Terry") and [Brownie McGhee](/wiki/Brownie_McGhee "Brownie McGhee").[[4]](#cite_note-:10-4) Eventually, [Robert Lockwood](/wiki/Robert_Lockwood_Jr. "Robert Lockwood Jr.") advised Thorogood to get an electric guitar,[[4]](#cite_note-:10-4) and in December 1973, he formed a band, "the Delaware Destroyers", with high school friend and drummer Jeff Simon.[[2]](#cite_note-web_bio-2)[[9]](#cite_note-:5-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) With additional players, the Delaware Destroyers developed its sound, a mixture of [Chicago blues](/wiki/Chicago_blues "Chicago blues") and [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll").[[7]](#cite_note-Erlewine-7) The band's first shows were in the [Rathskeller](/wiki/Rathskeller "Rathskeller") bar at the [University of Delaware](/wiki/University_of_Delaware "University of Delaware") and at [Deer Park Tavern](/wiki/Deer_Park_Tavern "Deer Park Tavern"), both in [Newark, Delaware](/wiki/Newark,_Delaware "Newark, Delaware").[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) Eventually, the band's name was shortened to just "the Destroyers".

Thorogood recorded a 20-song demo in 1974;[[13]](#cite_note-:172-13) 10 of those songs were eventually released as *[Better Than The Rest](/wiki/Better_Than_the_Rest "Better Than the Rest")* by [MCA Records](/wiki/MCA_Records "MCA Records").[[14]](#cite_note-:02-14) John Forward discovered the band in 1975, and helped them secure a recording contract with [Rounder Records](/wiki/Rounder_Records "Rounder Records").[[8]](#cite_note-:4-8) The band's major recording debut came with the album *[George Thorogood and the Destroyers](/wiki/George_Thorogood_and_the_Destroyers_(album) "George Thorogood and the Destroyers (album)"),* which was released on August 16, 1977.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The next year, Thorogood released his next album with the Destroyers titled *[Move It on Over](/wiki/Move_It_On_Over_(album) "Move It On Over (album)")*, which included a remake of [Hank Williams](/wiki/Hank_Williams "Hank Williams")'s "[Move It on Over](/wiki/Move_It_On_Over_(song) "Move It On Over (song)")", and a reworking of the [Bo Diddley](/wiki/Bo_Diddley "Bo Diddley") song "[Who Do You Love](/wiki/Who_Do_You_Love%3F_(Bo_Diddley_song) "Who Do You Love? (Bo Diddley song)")", both released as singles in 1978 and 1979 respectively.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) In 1980, rhythm guitarist Ron "Roadblock" Smith left the band, and was replaced by saxophonist Hank "Hurricane" Carter, who first played with the band on their fourth studio album, *[More George Thorogood and the Destroyers](/wiki/More_George_Thorogood_and_the_Destroyers "More George Thorogood and the Destroyers").[[18]](#cite_note-:022-18)* The band's early success contributed to the rise of folk label [Rounder Records](/wiki/Rounder_Records "Rounder Records").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

During the late 1970s, Thorogood and his band were based in [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"). He was friends with [Jimmy Thackery](/wiki/Jimmy_Thackery "Jimmy Thackery") of the [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")–based blues band, [The Nighthawks](/wiki/The_Nighthawks "The Nighthawks"). While touring in the 1970s, the Destroyers and the Nighthawks were playing shows in [Georgetown](/wiki/Georgetown_(Washington,_D.C.) "Georgetown (Washington, D.C.)") at venues across the street from each other. The Destroyers were engaged at the [Cellar Door](/wiki/The_Cellar_Door "The Cellar Door") and the Nighthawks at Desperados. At midnight, while both bands played [Elmore James](/wiki/Elmore_James "Elmore James")'s "[Madison Blues](/wiki/Madison_Blues "Madison Blues")" in the same key, Thorogood and Thackery left their clubs, met in the middle of M Street, exchanged guitar cords and went on to play with the opposite band in the other club.[[20]](#cite_note-20) The connection with the Nighthawks was extended further when Nighthawks bass player Jan Zukowski supported Thorogood's set with [Bo Diddley](/wiki/Bo_Diddley "Bo Diddley") and [Albert Collins](/wiki/Albert_Collins "Albert Collins") at the [Live Aid](/wiki/Live_Aid "Live Aid") concert in [Philadelphia, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Philadelphia,_Pennsylvania "Philadelphia, Pennsylvania"), on July 13, 1985.[[21]](#cite_note-Larkin-21)

Thorogood gained his first mainstream exposure as a support act for [the Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones") during their [1981 U.S. tour](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones_American_Tour_1981 "The Rolling Stones American Tour 1981").[[21]](#cite_note-Larkin-21) During this time, Thorogood and the Destroyers became known for their rigorous touring schedule, including the "50/50" tour in 1981,[[22]](#cite_note-:0-22) on which the band toured all 50 US states in 50 days.[[23]](#cite_note-WGN-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) After two shows in [Boulder, Colorado](/wiki/Boulder,_Colorado "Boulder, Colorado"), Thorogood and his band flew to Hawaii for one show and then performed a show in [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska") the following night. The next day, Thorogood and his band met his roadies in Washington and continued the one-show-per-state tour. In addition, he played Washington, D.C., on the same day that he performed a show in Maryland, thereby playing 51 shows in 50 days.[[18]](#cite_note-:022-18)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/George_Thorogood_WPC.jpg/250px-George_Thorogood_WPC.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_Thorogood_WPC.jpg)

Thorogood performing at [William Paterson College](/wiki/William_Paterson_University "William Paterson University") in [Wayne, New Jersey](/wiki/Wayne,_New_Jersey "Wayne, New Jersey"), in April 1986

With his contract with Rounder Records expiring, Thorogood signed with [EMI America Records](/wiki/EMI_America_Records "EMI America Records")[[25]](#cite_note-Certs82-25) and in 1982, released *[Bad to the Bone](/wiki/Bad_to_the_Bone_(George_Thorogood_and_the_Destroyers_album) "Bad to the Bone (George Thorogood and the Destroyers album)")*, which went [gold](/wiki/Recording_Industry_Association_of_America "Recording Industry Association of America").[[26]](#cite_note-:8-26) The album's [title track](/wiki/Bad_to_the_Bone "Bad to the Bone") eventually became the band's most well-known song[[27]](#cite_note-27) through appearances on [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV") and use in films, television and commercials.[[28]](#cite_note-:3-28) Later that year he was the featured musical guest on *[Saturday Night Live](/wiki/Saturday_Night_Live "Saturday Night Live")* (Season 8, Episode 2) on the October 2, 1982, broadcast.[[29]](#cite_note-29) The next year, the Destroyers embarked on their "Around the World in 80 Days Tour", during which they toured Australia, New Zealand, Japan, Europe and the US, but the tour had to be ended early because of a riot at a festival in Spain.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-:7-31) Later that year, Thorogood released his only Christmas song, "[Rock and Roll Christmas](/wiki/Rock_and_Roll_Christmas_(song) "Rock and Roll Christmas (song)")".[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-:13-33) It was written by Thorogood[[34]](#cite_note-34) as a holiday special for [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV"), with [John Lee Hooker](/wiki/John_Lee_Hooker "John Lee Hooker") appearing in the music video.[[35]](#cite_note-35) In 1984 Thorogood appeared in the music video for the song "[All My Rowdy Friends Are Coming Over Tonight](/wiki/All_My_Rowdy_Friends_Are_Coming_Over_Tonight "All My Rowdy Friends Are Coming Over Tonight")" by American singer-songwriter and musician [Hank Williams Jr](/wiki/Hank_Williams_Jr. "Hank Williams Jr.").[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)

His sixth studio album was *[Maverick](/wiki/Maverick_(George_Thorogood_and_the_Destroyers_album) "Maverick (George Thorogood and the Destroyers album)")*, released in January 1985.[[38]](#cite_note-RPM-Feb-23-38) The album featured Thorogood's only song to reach the *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* [Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100"), a remake of [Johnny Otis](/wiki/Johnny_Otis "Johnny Otis")'s "[Willie and the Hand Jive](/wiki/Willie_and_the_Hand_Jive "Willie and the Hand Jive")", which peaked at number 63,[[39]](#cite_note-39) and his concert staple "[I Drink Alone](/wiki/I_Drink_Alone "I Drink Alone")". During this time Thorogood became heavily interested in [country music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music"). He intended to record a country album, but never did.[[40]](#cite_note-40) on July 13, 1985, the Destroyers performed at [Live Aid](/wiki/Live_Aid "Live Aid") at the [John F. Kennedy Stadium](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_Stadium "John F. Kennedy Stadium") in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia").[[21]](#cite_note-Larkin-21) The Destroyers were only invited to play the day before after [Tears For Fears](/wiki/Tears_for_Fears "Tears for Fears") cancelled. Bo Diddley and Albert Collins also played with them.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) EMI America released Thorogood's first live album, titled *[Live](/wiki/Live_(George_Thorogood_and_the_Destroyers_album) "Live (George Thorogood and the Destroyers album)")*, in August 1986.[[43]](#cite_note-:03-43) The album was one of Thorogood's most successful, eventually being certified platinum by the [RIAA](/wiki/Recording_Industry_Association_of_America "Recording Industry Association of America").[[26]](#cite_note-:8-26) In 1988, rhythm guitarist Steve Chrismar joined the band, and was featured on their next album, *[Born to Be Bad](/wiki/Born_to_Be_Bad_(album) "Born to Be Bad (album)")*, also released in 1988.[[18]](#cite_note-:022-18) That summer he also toured with American musician [Brian Setzer](/wiki/Brian_Setzer "Brian Setzer").[[44]](#cite_note-44) Thorogood and the Destroyers were at the height of their popularity during the 80's, with their 4 albums with EMI selling a combined total of 3.5 million copies.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

Thorogood's popularity began to decline in the 1990s. His eighth studio album, *[Boogie People](/wiki/Boogie_People "Boogie People")*, only sold around 300,000 copies,[[46]](#cite_note-:1-46) but it did contain the song "[If You Don't Start Drinkin' (I'm Gonna Leave)](/wiki/If_You_Don%27t_Start_Drinkin%27 "If You Don't Start Drinkin'")", which eventually became a concert staple.[[18]](#cite_note-:022-18) That year he also toured with [Elvin Bishop](/wiki/Elvin_Bishop "Elvin Bishop") in support of Bishop's album *[Don't Let the Bossman Get You Down!](/wiki/Don%27t_Let_the_Bossman_Get_You_Down! "Don't Let the Bossman Get You Down!")*.[[47]](#cite_note-47) The next year, *[The Baddest of George Thorogood and the Destroyers](/wiki/The_Baddest_of_George_Thorogood_and_the_Destroyers "The Baddest of George Thorogood and the Destroyers")* was released,[[18]](#cite_note-:022-18) it was the band's second and final album to be certified platinum by the RIAA.[[26]](#cite_note-:8-26) His next album, *[Haircut](/wiki/Haircut_(album) "Haircut (album)")*, contained the song "[Get a Haircut](/wiki/Get_a_Haircut "Get a Haircut")", which peaked at No. 2 on the *Billboard* [Album Rock Tracks](/wiki/Album_Rock_Tracks "Album Rock Tracks"),[[48]](#cite_note-Billboard-48) and was the No. 1 most played song on Canadian FM radio.[[49]](#cite_note-49) In April 1995, EMI released the Destroyer's second live album, *[Live: Let's Work Together](/wiki/Live:_Let%27s_Work_Together "Live: Let's Work Together")*.[[50]](#cite_note-50) After the release of the album the Destroyers embarked on a summer tour, after which they began preparing material for their next album.[[51]](#cite_note-51) The Destroyers' next album was 1997's *[Rockin' My Life Away](/wiki/Rockin%27_My_Life_Away "Rockin' My Life Away")*. It was their first studio album to miss the [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200") since their debut in 1977, although it was their first to chart on *Billboard's* Top Blues Album chart.[[52]](#cite_note-:16-52)

In 1998 EMI America dropped the Destroyers, so they signed with [CMC International](/wiki/CMC_International "CMC International").[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54) The next year, they released *[Half a Boy/Half a Man](/wiki/Half_a_Boy/Half_a_Man "Half a Boy/Half a Man")*.[[18]](#cite_note-:022-18) The album failed to chart in any capacity, and its single, "I Don't Trust Nobody", was their final single to chart, peaking at number 24 on the *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* Mainstream Rock chart.[[52]](#cite_note-:16-52) The same year they were the featured musical guest on *[Late Night with Conan O'Brien](/wiki/Late_Night_with_Conan_O%27Brien "Late Night with Conan O'Brien")* on the June 8 broadcast.[[55]](#cite_note-55) CMC International also released their third live album, *Live in '99*.[[56]](#cite_note-56) Their next albums all charted high on *Billboard's* Top Blues Album chart.[[52]](#cite_note-:16-52)

George Thorogood signed a three-album deal with [Eagle Records](/wiki/Eagle_Records "Eagle Records") in 2002,[[57]](#cite_note-57) and released *[Ride 'Til I Die](/wiki/Ride_%27Til_I_Die "Ride 'Til I Die")* the next year.[[8]](#cite_note-:4-8) *Ride 'Til I Die* was the final Destroyers album to feature Hank "Hurricane" Carter, he was replaced by Buddy Leach.[[18]](#cite_note-:022-18) In 2004, *[Greatest Hits: 30 Years of Rock](/wiki/Greatest_Hits:_30_Years_of_Rock "Greatest Hits: 30 Years of Rock")* was released,[[18]](#cite_note-:022-18) it was the band's final album to be certified gold by the RIAA.[[26]](#cite_note-:8-26) Eagle released their fourth live album, *[30th Anniversary Tour: Live](/wiki/30th_Anniversary_Tour:_Live "30th Anniversary Tour: Live")*, also in 2004,[[58]](#cite_note-58) and *[The Hard Stuff](/wiki/The_Hard_Stuff_(George_Thorogood_and_the_Destroyers_album) "The Hard Stuff (George Thorogood and the Destroyers album)")* in 2006.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

The Destroyers toured with Buddy Guy in the summer of 2008,[[60]](#cite_note-60) before returning to EMI/Capitol in 2009 to release their next studio album, *[The Dirty Dozen](/wiki/The_Dirty_Dozen_(album) "The Dirty Dozen (album)")*.[[8]](#cite_note-:4-8) It was their first studio album since *Haircut* to reach the [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200"), peaking at number 169.[[61]](#cite_note-61) In 2011 they released their most recent studio album, *[2120 South Michigan Ave.](/wiki/2120_South_Michigan_Ave. "2120 South Michigan Ave.")*, which was a tribute to [Chess Record](/wiki/Chess_Records "Chess Records") artists.[[5]](#cite_note-gw-5) In 2012, Thorogood was named one of the "50 Most Influential Delawareans of the Past 50 Years".[[62]](#cite_note-62) The next year the Destroyers celebrated their 40th year anniversary as a band with a national tour.[[63]](#cite_note-:9-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64) Thorogood released his first proper solo album in 2017, titled *[Party of One](/wiki/Party_of_One_(George_Thorogood_album) "Party of One (George Thorogood album)"),*[[65]](#cite_note-:12-65) which was his best selling album in a long time.[[9]](#cite_note-:5-9) On April 21, 2018,[[66]](#cite_note-66) the Destroyers released the single "Shot Down" for [Record Store Day](/wiki/Record_Store_Day "Record Store Day").[[67]](#cite_note-67)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/George_Thorogood_MMP_Music_Award.jpg/250px-George_Thorogood_MMP_Music_Award.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_Thorogood_MMP_Music_Award.jpg)

Thorogood (center) receives the MMP Music Award and Hall of Fame Honors with William Lewis IV, Alexis Paige, Joseph W. Clark, and Kevin Edwards

On March 14, 2020, Thorogood was inducted into the Mississippi Music Project Hall of Fame in Biloxi, Mississippi, and was awarded the MMP Music Award for his lifelong commitment to the music industry,[[68]](#cite_note-68) by Joseph W. Clark.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Hank Carter, who served as the saxophonist for Thorogood's band from 1980 to 2003, died on September 14, 2021, at the age of 71.[[69]](#cite_note-auto-69)

On April 11, 2023, Thorogood's social media pages announced that he was seriously ill, although the nature of his illness was not disclosed. Tour dates from April 27 to May 21 were all cancelled.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

In February 2025, just before Thorogood's 75th birthday, the band launched their "Baddest Show on Earth Tour", which would take them across several states and Australia.[[71]](#cite_note-:6-71) A live album, titled *The Baddest Show on Earth: Greatest Hits Live* was announced for release on June 12, 2026, featuring previously unreleased performances from 1978 through 2024.[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73) A live version of "Who Do You Love?" was released as a single.[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=George_Thorogood&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Personal life")]

George Thorogood married Marla Raderman on July 16, 1985.[[76]](#cite_note-76) She died from [ovarian cancer](/wiki/Ovarian_cancer "Ovarian cancer") in 2019.[[77]](#cite_note-77) They have one daughter, Rio Thorogood.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

Thorogood has been a baseball fan[[23]](#cite_note-WGN-23) for most of his life, playing semi-pro ball as a second baseman during the 1970s (drummer Jeff Simon played center field on the same team). He took his daughter to Chicago for her first major league game ([Cubs](/wiki/Chicago_Cubs "Chicago Cubs") vs. [Rockies](/wiki/Colorado_Rockies "Colorado Rockies")), during which he sang "[Take Me Out to the Ball Game](/wiki/Take_Me_Out_to_the_Ball_Game "Take Me Out to the Ball Game")". In a 2011 *[Guitar World](/wiki/Guitar_World "Guitar World")* interview, he stated "I'm a [Mets](/wiki/New_York_Mets "New York Mets") fan. There aren't many of us but you know, that's me."[[5]](#cite_note-gw-5)

In April 2023, Thorogood had to cancel the first leg of his 50th anniversary tour due to a serious medical condition.[[79]](#cite_note-79)
